name: Diane -- Maintenance Manager
role: Maintenance Manager at a Gulf Coast refinery. Mechanical engineering background with 12 years in maintenance execution. Reports to the Plant Manager. Manages 4 maintenance supervisors and ~30 technicians across mechanical, electrical, and instrumentation crafts.
goals: Hit 90%+ schedule compliance this quarter (currently at 81%)
Keep the PM backlog under 2 weeks of work per technician
Reduce emergency/reactive work from 18% to below 10% of total work
Get work orders closed with proper failure codes so reliability can do their analysis
Keep overtime under budget while covering both scheduled and emergency work
frustrations: K-101 trips at 2 AM and now the entire day's schedule is blown. Three people pulled off planned work for emergency response.
Two critical work orders open for K-101 and one is unassigned because the only bearing specialist is already on T-401's combustion inspection.
Parts availability. The lube oil filter for K-101 should have been in stock but it was consumed 3 months ago and never reordered.
Reliability opens cases and investigations but the corrective actions turn into work orders that land on her already overloaded schedule.
Technicians close work orders with 'fixed it' and no details. She has to chase them down to get proper failure codes and notes.
Does not need MTBF charts or root cause trees. Needs to know: who is available, what's the priority, and do we have the parts.
behaviors: Runs the 7:00 AM morning meeting with supervisors, operations, and planning
First thing: checks what emergency work orders came in overnight
Reviews today's schedule against available craft resources
Reassigns people when emergencies disrupt the plan
Checks PM compliance weekly (are PMs getting done on time?)
Monitors backlog size and aging (overdue work orders are a red flag)
Approves overtime and contractor callouts when resources are short
Tracks maintenance budget spend vs plan monthly
tools_used: CMMS/EAM (work order management, scheduling, backlog, resource allocation)
APM platform (reads Plant Overview for overnight events, does not use RCA or asset strategy modules)
Radio/phone (coordinating with supervisors and techs in the field)
primary_screens: Plant Overview (morning check: what broke overnight, what's scheduled today)
Work Orders (her primary screen: open, in progress, overdue, assignments)
Investigations (reads case status to anticipate incoming work orders from reliability)
time_horizon: Today to next week. Tactical. Execution-focused.
core_question: Who is fixing it, do they have what they need, and when will it be done?
quote: I do not need to know why K-101 keeps failing. That is Carlos's job. I need to know that Sarah is on the bearing inspection, the lube oil filter is in the warehouse, and she will be done by noon. If she is not, I need to know now so I can get someone else.
